title: Japanese stab binding, yotsume
subtitle: The four-hole stab stitch for side-sewn booklets
excerpt: Yotsume toji is the simplest of the Japanese stab-binding stitches: four holes, one thread path, a binding that works in a day with paper, an awl, and a single length of thread. The pages do not open flat, but the structure is fast, durable, and immediately readable as a hand-made artefact.
sourceNotes: Japanese stab-binding forms documented in late-Meiji papercraft and bookbinding manuals held in the Internet Archive (ja.archive.org). The yotsume toji (four-hole stitch) is the canonical beginner stab binding in the tradition. Cross-referenced with Douglas Cockerell, Bookbinding and the Care of Books (1901), appendix on Eastern binding forms.

content.text: Stab binding is the Japanese tradition of sewing through the entire text block side-on, entering through holes near the spine edge rather than through folded signatures. The

, is the entry point in the tradition. The stitch pattern is a straightforward loop: up through each hole from back to front, along the spine edge from hole to hole, and back down. The result is a visible thread path along the spine and a book that sits open at approximately 90°.

attrs.body: Cut the text block in two or three passes against a metal ruler; change the blade when it drags rather than slices.
attrs.title: Cutting tools

items.qty: As many sheets as required, no maximum page count, but 40 to 80 pages handles well
items.name: Text-block paper, 90 gsm, A5 or smaller

items.qty: 2 pieces, front and back, cut to the same dimensions as the text block
items.name: Cover paper, cardstock or washi-kozo

items.qty: Approximately 4× the height of the book
items.name: Waxed linen bookbinding thread, 4-cord

items.qty: For holding the block while piercing
items.name: Bulldog clips or book press

content.text: Collate the text-block sheets in order and tap the block on the table to align all edges. Place the covers front and back. Clamp with bulldog clips at the foredge to hold the block square.

Mark four holes on the spine edge of the block. Using the metal ruler and pencil, mark from the head end: hole A at 5 mm from the head; hole B at 25 mm from the head; hole C at 25 mm from the tail; hole D at 5 mm from the tail. For an A5 block (210 mm tall), holes B and C will be approximately 160 mm apart. Mark the positions on the front cover through to the back with the ruler.
Pierce all four holes with the awl, working from front to back. Drive straight through perpendicular to the spine edge. Keep the block clamped throughout.
Wax the thread. Thread the needle. Do not knot the end, the thread is anchored by the stitch pattern itself.

Begin at hole A from the back. Bring the needle up through hole A front to back, leaving a 10 cm tail at the front. Loop the thread around the spine edge above hole A and back down through hole A again (this wrap anchors the start). Bring the thread along the front face to hole B. Enter through B back to front.

Loop around the spine edge at hole B and back through hole B. Continue along the front to hole C; enter through C from front to back; loop the tail edge and back through C. Continue to hole D; enter from back to front; loop the head edge and back through hole D.

Return from hole D back along the front face to hole C, B, A, passing through each hole in reverse and looping the spine edge each time. At hole A, tie the thread to the initial tail with two half-hitches inside the book. Trim both tails to 5 mm and tuck against the spine edge.

attrs.intro: Problems that arise in yotsume toji.

items.fix: Re-pierce the holes with the awl from the front face with a slightly wider motion to open the channel. Use a needle with an eye and shaft width matched to the thread.
items.cause: The awl hole is too small for the thread, and the needle is forcing its way through rather than passing cleanly.
items.symptom: Holes tear when sewing through a thick block

items.fix: After each spine-edge wrap, hold the thread against the front face with a finger while pulling the needle through the next hole.
items.cause: Thread tension was not held while moving between holes.
items.symptom: The thread wraps on the spine are loose and the book looks sloppy

attrs.heading: Common problems

content.text: Once the four-hole stitch reads cleanly, the hemp-leaf and kikko patterns add complexity.
